---
title: "Editing Your Content"
subtitle: "Personalizing your homepage with text, images, and a live calendar"
---

## Overview

Now that you understand how the files are organized, it's time to make the website yours.
This chapter focuses on editing `index.qmd` — your homepage — which is the first thing
visitors will see.

You'll learn how to:

- Replace the placeholder text with your own name and bio
- Add your own profile photo
- Arrange text and images side by side using columns
- Embed a live public Google Calendar

::: {.callout-tip}
Before making any edits, it's a good habit to run `quarto preview` in the Terminal so
the preview is live in your browser. That way you can see your changes reflected in
real time as you save the file.
:::

---

## Step 1: Open Your Homepage File

In RStudio, find `index.qmd` in the **Files** panel (bottom-right) and click on it to
open it in the editor.

At the very top of the file you'll see the **YAML front matter** — a short block between
two `---` lines that sets page-level options:
```yaml
---
title: "Vanessa Quintana"
subtitle: "Applied Ecological Modeler • Coastal Systems Scientist • Geospatial Analyst"
page-layout: full
image: images/Author_Picture.jpg
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    smooth-scroll: true
---
```

Start by updating these fields:

- **`title`** — Replace `"Vanessa Quintana"` with your own name
- **`subtitle`** — Replace the subtitle with your own roles or research focus areas,
  separated by `•` if you'd like the same style
- **`image`** — This points to your profile photo (more on this in Step 2)

::: {.callout-note}
The `page-layout: full` setting gives the homepage a wider layout than the other pages,
which is why it looks different from `research.qmd` or `cv.qmd`. Leave this as-is for
the homepage.
:::

---

## Step 2: Add Your Profile Photo

Your profile photo is displayed on the left side of the homepage. To replace the
placeholder with your own image:

1. Find a photo you'd like to use — a headshot or professional photo works best
2. Rename the file to something simple with no spaces, for example `my_photo.jpg`
3. Move or copy the file into the **`images/`** folder inside your project
4. In `index.qmd`, find the line that references the image:
```markdown
![](images/Author_Picture.jpg){.profile-image width="100%" fig-alt="Vanessa Quintana"}
```

5. Replace `Author_Picture.jpg` with your own filename, and update the `fig-alt` text
   with your name:
```markdown
![](images/my_photo.jpg){.profile-image width="100%" fig-alt="Your Name"}
```

::: {.callout-tip}
The `fig-alt` text is the **alt text** for your image — a short description that appears
if the image fails to load and that screen readers use for accessibility. Always include
a brief, accurate description.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
Make sure your image file is actually inside the `images/` folder and that the filename
in your `.qmd` file matches exactly — including capitalization and the file extension
(`.jpg`, `.png`, etc.). A mismatch here is one of the most common beginner errors.
:::

---

## Step 3: Edit Your Bio Text

Below the YAML front matter, the homepage is built using **columns** — a Quarto layout
feature that lets you place content side by side. Here's how the two-column layout is
structured in `index.qmd`:
```markdown
::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="32%"}

  [your photo and profile panel go here]

:::
::: {.column width="68%"}

  [your bio text goes here]

:::
:::
```

The first column (32% wide) holds your photo and badge list. The second column (68% wide)
holds your bio and research philosophy. To update your bio, find the text in the wider
column and replace it with your own:
```markdown
::: {.column width="68%"}

::: {.kicker}
About My Work
:::

I work at the intersection of [your research areas here]. My research focuses on
[what you study and why it matters].

I approach [your methods or philosophy here].

:::
```

::: {.callout-note}
**What is `.kicker`?** The `.kicker` is a small styled label that appears above a
section heading — like a category tag. It's defined in `styles.css` and gives the page
a clean, editorial look. You can change the text inside it to anything you like, or
remove it entirely if you prefer.
:::

---

## Step 4: Understanding Side-by-Side Layouts (Columns)

The column layout you just saw is one of the most useful features in Quarto for building
a polished homepage. Here's the full pattern so you can use it anywhere on your site:
```markdown
::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

Content for the left side goes here.

You can put text, images, lists, or anything else.

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

Content for the right side goes here.

:::
:::
```

The widths must add up to 100%. You can split them however you like — for example
`30%` / `70%`, or `25%` / `25%` / `25%` / `25%` for four equal columns (as used in
the Research Areas section of the homepage).

### Placing an Image Next to Text

A common use of the column layout is putting a photo or figure next to a block of text.
Here's a simple example you can copy and adapt:
```markdown
::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}

![](images/my_photo.jpg){width="100%" fig-alt="A description of the image"}

:::
::: {.column width="60%"}

### A heading for the text side

Write your paragraph text here. This will appear to the right of the image.
You can include as much or as little text as you need.

:::
:::
```

::: {.callout-tip}
Add a blank line between the `:::` dividers and your content. Quarto needs that spacing
to correctly parse the column layout — missing blank lines is a common cause of
formatting issues.
:::

---

## Step 5: Update Your Badge List

In the left column of the homepage, below your photo, there is a **badge list** — a
set of small styled labels showing your affiliation, title, or research focus areas.
Find this block in `index.qmd`:
```markdown
::: {.badge-list}
Marine Sciences, University of Maine

ORISE Fellow, U.S. Army Corps of Engineers

Estuarine Hydrodynamics and Sediment Transport

Agent-Based and Ecological Modeling
:::
```

Replace each line with your own affiliations, department, institution, or keywords. Each
separate line becomes its own badge. You can add or remove lines as needed.

---

## Step 6: Embed a Public Google Calendar

If you have a public Google Calendar — for office hours, events, lab meetings, or
workshops — you can embed it directly into your website as a live, interactive calendar.
Any updates you make in Google Calendar will automatically appear on your site.

### First: Make Your Google Calendar Public

1. Go to [calendar.google.com](https://calendar.google.com) and sign in
2. In the left panel, find the calendar you want to embed, click the **three dots** next
   to its name, and select **"Settings and sharing"**
3. Under **"Access permissions for events"**, check the box that says
   **"Make available to public"**
4. Confirm the warning if prompted

### Second: Get the Embed Code

1. Still on the calendar settings page, scroll down to **"Integrate calendar"**
2. Copy the **"Public URL to this calendar"** — it will look like a long link ending
   in `@group.calendar.google.com`
3. In the same section, click **"Customize"** to open the embed builder if you'd like
   to adjust the calendar's appearance, then copy the `<iframe>` embed code

### Third: Add the Calendar to Your Site (*optional*)

Create a new file in your project called `calendar.qmd` and add the following. Replace
the `src` URL with your own calendar's embed URL:
```markdown
---
title: "Event Calendar"
toc: true
page-layout: article
---

# Event Calendar

<div style="
  max-width: 1100px;
  margin: 0 auto 50px auto;
  border-radius: 22px;
  background: #ffffff;
  box-shadow: 0 12px 25px rgba(0,0,0,0.15);
  overflow: hidden;
">

  <iframe 
    src="YOUR-GOOGLE-CALENDAR-EMBED-URL-HERE"
    style="border:0; width:100%; height:900px; display:block;"
    frameborder="0"
    scrolling="no"
  ></iframe>

</div>
```

### Fourth: Add the Calendar to Your Navigation Bar (*optional*)

Open `_quarto.yml` and add your new calendar page to the navbar under the `left:`
section:
```yaml
navbar:
  left:
    - href: calendar.qmd
      text: Calendar
    - href: research.qmd
      text: Research
```

Save `_quarto.yml`, then run `quarto preview` in the Terminal to see your calendar
page live.

::: {.callout-note}
The calendar will only display correctly when viewed in a browser — it won't show up
in RStudio's built-in viewer panel. If you don't see it in the RStudio preview pane,
copy the local address (e.g. `http://localhost:4321`) and open it in Chrome or Firefox.
:::

---

## Step 7: Save and Preview Your Changes

After making any edits in `index.qmd` or `_quarto.yml`, save the file with
**Ctrl+S** (Windows) or **Cmd+S** (Mac). If `quarto preview` is already running in the
Terminal, your browser should refresh automatically within a few seconds.

If the preview doesn't update, go to the Terminal and run:
```bash
quarto preview
```

::: {.callout-important}
If you see an error in the Terminal after saving, read the message carefully — Quarto's
error messages usually tell you exactly which file and line the problem is on. The most
common issues are mismatched `:::` column dividers, incorrect image filenames, or
indentation errors in `_quarto.yml`.
:::

---

## Other Resources

As you continue building your website, you may want to explore more advanced customization, layouts, and templates. The official Quarto documentation is extremely well organized and worth bookmarking.

::: {.callout-tip}
If something feels limited in your website, it almost always already exists in the Quarto documentation — you just need to find the right section.
:::

---

### Website Development

- [Quarto Websites Documentation](https://quarto.org/docs/websites/)  
  A complete guide to building and structuring Quarto websites, including navigation, pages, and configuration.

- [Quarto Website Gallery](https://quarto.org/docs/gallery/#websites)  
  Real examples of websites built with Quarto. Useful for inspiration and design ideas.

- [Quarto Guide](https://quarto.org/docs/guide/)  
  A broader introduction to Quarto, including project types, publishing, and workflows.

---

### Customization and Advanced Formatting

- [HTML Format Reference](https://quarto.org/docs/reference/formats/html.html)  
  Detailed reference for all HTML output options, including themes, layout, and advanced settings.

- [HTML Basics](https://quarto.org/docs/output-formats/html-basics.html)  
  A simpler introduction to how Quarto renders HTML, useful for understanding how your site is structured.

---

::: {.callout-note}
You do not need to learn all of this at once. Start by building your site, then return to these resources when you want to customize something specific.
:::

---

### Suggested Workflow for Learning More

1. Build your basic website using this tutorial  
2. Identify one thing you want to improve (layout, styling, navigation)  
3. Use the documentation above to find that specific feature  
4. Implement and test it  

::: {.callout-important}
Avoid trying to learn all of Quarto at once. Focus on small, incremental improvements.
:::

---

## Summary: What You Edited in This Chapter

| What | Where | How |
|---|---|---|
| Your name and subtitle | `index.qmd` YAML front matter | Edit the `title` and `subtitle` fields |
| Your profile photo | `images/` folder + `index.qmd` | Add file to `images/`, update the filename |
| Your bio text | `index.qmd` wide column | Replace the paragraph text |
| Your badge labels | `index.qmd` `.badge-list` block | Replace each line with your own info |
| Side-by-side layout | Any `.qmd` file | Use the `{.columns}` / `{.column}` pattern |
| Google Calendar | New `calendar.qmd` + `_quarto.yml` | Embed iframe, add link to navbar |

In the next chapter, you'll edit the inner pages — Research, Publications, Projects,
and CV — and learn how to add your own work to each one.
